**Separatism** is the advocacy of cultural, ethnic, tribal, religious, racial, regional, governmental, or gender separation from the larger group. As with [secession](/wiki/Secession "Secession"), separatism conventionally refers to full political separation. Groups simply seeking greater autonomy are usually not considered separatists.[[1]](#cite_note-1) Some discourse settings equate separatism with [religious segregation](/wiki/Religious_segregation "Religious segregation"), [racial segregation](/wiki/Racial_segregation "Racial segregation"), or [sex segregation](/wiki/Sex_segregation "Sex segregation"), while other discourse settings take the broader view that separation by choice may serve useful purposes and is not the same as government-enforced segregation. There is some academic debate about this definition, and in particular how it relates to [secessionism](/wiki/Secession "Secession"), as has been discussed online.[[2]](#cite_note-2)

Separatist groups practice a form of [identity politics](/wiki/Identity_politics "Identity politics"), or political activity and theorizing founded in the shared experiences of the group's members. Such groups believe attempts at integration with dominant groups compromise their identity and ability to pursue greater [self-determination](/wiki/Self-determination "Self-determination").[[3]](#cite_note-Stanford-3) However, economic and political factors usually are critical in creating strong separatist movements as opposed to less ambitious identity movements.[[4]](#cite_note-Horowitz-4)

## Motivations

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Separatism&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: Motivations")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/7/75/Somunanacio11%282%29.jpg/250px-Somunanacio11%282%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:Somunanacio11(2).jpg)

Support for [Catalan independence](/wiki/Catalan_independence "Catalan independence") is based on the idea that [Catalonia](/wiki/Catalonia "Catalonia"), as a nation, has the right to the statehood.

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/a/a4/Hashim_Thaci_Joe_Biden_Fatmir_Sejdiu_with_Declaration_of_Independence_of_Kosovo.JPG/250px-Hashim_Thaci_Joe_Biden_Fatmir_Sejdiu_with_Declaration_of_Independence_of_Kosovo.JPG)](/wiki/File:Hashim_Thaci_Joe_Biden_Fatmir_Sejdiu_with_Declaration_of_Independence_of_Kosovo.JPG)

The former [KLA](/wiki/Kosovo_Liberation_Army "Kosovo Liberation Army") leader [Hashim Thaçi](/wiki/Hashim_Tha%C3%A7i "Hashim Thaçi") (left) and then-U.S. Vice President [Joe Biden](/wiki/Joe_Biden "Joe Biden") with [Declaration of Independence of Kosovo](/wiki/2008_Kosovo_declaration_of_independence "2008 Kosovo declaration of independence")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/7/79/Battle_of_Kenesaw_Mountian.01850u.jpg/250px-Battle_of_Kenesaw_Mountian.01850u.jpg)](/wiki/File:Battle_of_Kenesaw_Mountian.01850u.jpg)

In 1861, the [American Civil War](/wiki/American_Civil_War "American Civil War") started after a separatist movement of [southern US states](/wiki/Confederate_States_of_America "Confederate States of America") seceded from the [United States](/wiki/United_States "United States").

Groups may have one or more motivations for separation, including:[[5]](#cite_note-5)

* Emotional resentment and hatred of rival communities.
* Protection from [genocide](/wiki/Genocide "Genocide") and ethnic cleansing.
* Resistance by victims of oppression, including denigration of their language, culture or religion.
* Influence and propaganda by those inside and outside the region who hope to gain politically from intergroup conflict and hatred.
* Economic and political dominance of one group that does not share power and privilege in an egalitarian fashion.
* Economic motivations: seeking to end economic exploitation by more powerful group or, conversely, to escape economic redistribution from a richer to a poorer group.
* Preservation of threatened religious, language or other cultural tradition.
* Destabilization from one separatist movement giving rise to others.
* Geopolitical power vacuum from breakup of larger states or empires.
* Continuing fragmentation as more and more states break up.
* Feeling that the perceived nation was added to the larger state by illegitimate means.
* The perception that the state can no longer support one's own group or has betrayed their interests.
* Opposition to political decisions.

## Types

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Separatism&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: Types")]

[Ethnic](/wiki/Ethnic_group "Ethnic group") separatism can be based on cultural, linguistic as well as [religious](/wiki/Religion "Religion") or [racial](/wiki/Race_(human_categorization) "Race (human categorization)") differences. Ethnic separatist movements were relevant since they represented historical delineations between [states](/wiki/State_(polity) "State (polity)"), or in recent times, were the cause of conflicts between peoples in Europe, Africa and Asia with different ethnic/linguistic origins.

### Gender separatism

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Separatism&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: Gender separatism")]

The relationship between gender and separatism is complex.[[6]](#cite_note-6) [Feminist separatism](/wiki/Feminist_separatism "Feminist separatism") is women's choosing to separate from ostensibly male-defined, male-dominated institutions, relationships, roles and activities.[[7]](#cite_note-7) [Lesbian separatism](/wiki/Lesbian_separatism "Lesbian separatism") advocates [lesbianism](/wiki/Lesbian "Lesbian") as the logical result of feminism. Some separatist feminists and lesbian separatists have chosen to live apart in [intentional community](/wiki/Intentional_community "Intentional community"), [cooperatives](/wiki/Cooperatives "Cooperatives"), and on [land trusts](/wiki/Land_trust "Land trust").[[8]](#cite_note-8) [Queer nationalism](/wiki/Queer_nationalism "Queer nationalism") (or "Gay separatism") seeks a community distinct and separate from other social groups.[[9]](#cite_note-9)[[10]](#cite_note-10) On the other hand, the [MGTOW](/wiki/Men_Going_Their_Own_Way "Men Going Their Own Way") movement is sometimes considered a [male-gender](/wiki/Masculine "Masculine") separatism, as at the center of this ideology is the notion of male separatism where men should not be a part of a feminist-biased society. Some fringe elements even propose a utopical no-women state.[[11]](#cite_note-11)[[12]](#cite_note-12)[[13]](#cite_note-13)

### Racial separatism

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Separatism&action=edit&section=6 "Edit section: Racial separatism")]

|  |  |
| --- | --- |
| Globe icon. | The examples and perspective in this section **deal primarily with the United States and do not represent a [worldwide view](/wiki/Wikipedia:WikiProject_Countering_systemic_bias "Wikipedia:WikiProject Countering systemic bias") of the subject**. You may [improve this section](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=Separatism&action=edit), discuss the issue on the [talk page](/wiki/Talk:Separatism "Talk:Separatism"), or create a new section, as appropriate. *(August 2011)* *([Learn how and when to remove this message](/wiki/Help:Maintenance_template_removal "Help:Maintenance template removal"))* |

Some separatist groups seek to separate from others along [racial](/wiki/Race_(human_classification) "Race (human classification)") lines. They oppose interracial marriage and integration with other races and seek separate schools, businesses, churches and other institutions, and often separate societies, territories, countries, and governments:

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/4/41/Aztl%C3%A1n_in_United_States_%28US48%29.svg/250px-Aztl%C3%A1n_in_United_States_%28US48%29.svg.png)](/wiki/File:Aztl%C3%A1n_in_United_States_(US48).svg)

Territories considered for "Aztlán"

* [Black separatism](/wiki/Black_separatism "Black separatism") (also known as [black nationalism](/wiki/Black_nationalism "Black nationalism")) is the most prominent wave advancing the concepts of "Black racial identity" in the United States and has been advanced by black leaders like [Marcus Garvey](/wiki/Marcus_Garvey "Marcus Garvey") and organizations such as the [Nation of Islam](/wiki/Nation_of_Islam "Nation of Islam"). [Critical race theorists](/wiki/Critical_race_theory "Critical race theory") like [New York University](/wiki/New_York_University "New York University")'s [Derrick Bell](/wiki/Derrick_Bell "Derrick Bell") and [University of Colorado](/wiki/University_of_Colorado_at_Boulder "University of Colorado at Boulder")'s [Richard Delgado](/wiki/Richard_Delgado "Richard Delgado") argue that US legal, education and political systems are rife with blatant racism. They support efforts like "all-black" schools and dorms and question the [efficacy](https://en.wiktionary.org/wiki/efficacy "wikt:efficacy") and merit of government-enforced integration.[[14]](#cite_note-14) In 2008 statements by Barack Obama's former pastor [Jeremiah Wright, Jr.](/wiki/Jeremiah_Wright,_Jr. "Jeremiah Wright, Jr."), revived the issue of the current relevance of black separatism.[[15]](#cite_note-15)
* [Latin American](/wiki/Latin_American "Latin American") concepts of racial identity such as the [bronze race](/wiki/Bronze_race "Bronze race") and [La Raza Cósmica](/wiki/La_Raza_C%C3%B3smica "La Raza Cósmica") are found in the small separatist [Raza Unida Party](/wiki/Raza_Unida_Party "Raza Unida Party"). The [Chicano Movement](/wiki/Chicano_Movement "Chicano Movement") (or [Chicano nation](/wiki/Chicano_nationalism "Chicano nationalism")) in the United States sought to recreate [Aztlán](/wiki/Aztl%C3%A1n "Aztlán"), the mythical homeland of the [Aztecs](/wiki/Aztec "Aztec") comprising the [Southwestern United States](/wiki/Southwestern_United_States "Southwestern United States").[[16]](#cite_note-aztlan.net-16)
* [White separatism](/wiki/White_separatism "White separatism") in the United States and Western Europe seeks separation of the white race and limits to nonwhite [immigration](/wiki/Immigration "Immigration") under the argument that these policies are necessary for the white race's survival.

[Religious](/wiki/Religion "Religion") separatist groups and sects want to withdraw from some larger religious groups and/or believe they should interact primarily with coreligionists:[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

* [English Christians](/wiki/English_Dissenters "English Dissenters") in the 16th and 17th centuries who wished to separate from the [Church of England](/wiki/Church_of_England "Church of England") and form independent local churches were influential politically under [Oliver Cromwell](/wiki/Oliver_Cromwell "Oliver Cromwell"), who was himself a separatist. They were eventually called [Congregationalists](/wiki/Congregationalists "Congregationalists").[[17]](#cite_note-17) The [Pilgrims](/wiki/Pilgrim_(Plymouth_Colony) "Pilgrim (Plymouth Colony)") who established the first successful colony in [New England](/wiki/New_England "New England") were separatists.[[18]](#cite_note-18)
* Christian separatist groups in [Indonesia](/wiki/Maluku_Sovereignty_Front "Maluku Sovereignty Front"),[[19]](#cite_note-19)[[20]](#cite_note-20) India[[21]](#cite_note-21) and [South Carolina](/wiki/South_Carolina "South Carolina") (United States)[[22]](#cite_note-22)[[23]](#cite_note-23)
* [Zionism](/wiki/Zionism "Zionism") sought the creation of the [State of Israel](/wiki/State_of_Israel "State of Israel") as a [Jewish](/wiki/Jewish "Jewish") homeland, with separation from [gentile](/wiki/Gentile "Gentile") Palestinians. [Simon Dubnow](/wiki/Simon_Dubnow "Simon Dubnow"), who had mixed feelings toward Zionism, formulated [Jewish Autonomism](/wiki/Jewish_Autonomism "Jewish Autonomism"), which was adopted in eastern Europe by Jewish political parties such as the [Bund](/wiki/General_Jewish_Labour_Bund_in_Lithuania,_Poland_and_Russia "General Jewish Labour Bund in Lithuania, Poland and Russia") and his own [Folkspartei](/wiki/Folkspartei "Folkspartei") before World War II.[[24]](#cite_note-24) Zionism can also be seen as somewhat ethnic too, however, as its definition of who is Jewish has often included people of Jewish background who do not practice the Jewish religion. It is further complicated as some who had ancestors who converted to Judaism, such as some Ethiopian Jews, may not share ethnic history with the Jews, however, are considered to be so but not without debate.[[25]](#cite_note-lucotte1999-25)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/6/69/MILF_militant_lying_prone.jpg/250px-MILF_militant_lying_prone.jpg)](/wiki/File:MILF_militant_lying_prone.jpg)

[Moro Islamic Liberation Front](/wiki/Moro_Islamic_Liberation_Front "Moro Islamic Liberation Front") fighter in the Philippines

* The [Partition of the British Raj](/wiki/Partition_of_India "Partition of India") into [India](/wiki/India "India") and [Pakistan](/wiki/Pakistan "Pakistan") (later [Bangladesh](/wiki/Bangladesh "Bangladesh") as well) arose as a result of separatism on the part of Muslims.
* The demand for an independent Sikh homeland called [Khalistan](/wiki/Khalistan "Khalistan") emerged during the 1970s and 1980s amid political tensions in [Punjab](/wiki/Punjab,_India "Punjab, India"), particularly surrounding the [Anandpur Sahib Resolution](/wiki/Anandpur_Resolution "Anandpur Resolution"), which primarily sought greater autonomy for Punjab and the protection of Sikh identity within the Indian Union. Escalation occurred when armed militants led by Jarnail Singh Bhindranwale occupied the [Harimandir Sahib](/wiki/Harimandir_Sahib "Harimandir Sahib") (Golden Temple) complex. In June 1984, the Government of India launched [Operation Blue Star](/wiki/Operation_Blue_Star "Operation Blue Star") to remove the militants from the site. The military action resulted in casualties, including civilians, and caused deep anguish among many [Sikhs](/wiki/Sikh "Sikh") globally.

Later that year, the assassination of [Prime Minister](/wiki/Prime_Minister_of_India "Prime Minister of India") [Indira Gandhi](/wiki/Indira_Gandhi "Indira Gandhi") by her Sikh bodyguards led to the tragic [1984 anti-Sikh riots](/wiki/1984_anti-Sikh_riots "1984 anti-Sikh riots"), in which thousands of Sikhs were killed. While these events led to a rise in support for the [Khalistan movement](/wiki/Khalistan_movement "Khalistan movement") in some sections of the Sikh diaspora, the movement steadily declined in India by the 1990s.

Today, Sikhs in India are equal citizens under the Constitution, and actively contribute to the country's political, military, economic, and cultural life. The idea of Khalistan holds little to no support among Indian Sikhs, and is generally viewed as a fringe movement, largely sustained by diaspora activism abroad. Attempts to revive it within India have consistently failed to gain traction.[[26]](#cite_note-26)

* Muslim separatist groups in the [Philippines](/wiki/Philippines "Philippines") ([Mindanao](/wiki/Mindanao "Mindanao") and other regions: [Moro Islamic Liberation Front](/wiki/Moro_Islamic_Liberation_Front "Moro Islamic Liberation Front"), [Abu Sayyaf](/wiki/Abu_Sayyaf "Abu Sayyaf")), in [Thailand](/wiki/Thailand "Thailand") (see also [South Thailand insurgency](/wiki/Southern_Thailand_insurgency "Southern Thailand insurgency")), in [India](/wiki/India "India") (see also [Insurgency in Jammu and Kashmir](/wiki/Insurgency_in_Jammu_and_Kashmir "Insurgency in Jammu and Kashmir")), in the [People's Republic of China](/wiki/People%27s_Republic_of_China "People's Republic of China") ([Xinjiang](/wiki/Xinjiang "Xinjiang"): [East Turkestan Islamic Movement](/wiki/East_Turkestan_Islamic_Movement "East Turkestan Islamic Movement")), [Tanzania](/wiki/Tanzania "Tanzania") ([Zanzibarian](/wiki/Zanzibar "Zanzibar") separatist movements), in the [Central African Republic](/wiki/Central_African_Republic "Central African Republic") (Regions that are inhabited by Muslims: [Séléka](/wiki/S%C3%A9l%C3%A9ka "Séléka")), in [Russia](/wiki/Russia "Russia") (in the [Northern Caucasus](/wiki/Northern_Caucasus "Northern Caucasus"), especially in [Chechnya](/wiki/Chechnya "Chechnya"): [Caucasus Emirate](/wiki/Caucasus_Emirate "Caucasus Emirate")), in [Yugoslavia](/wiki/Yugoslavia "Yugoslavia") ([Bosnia and Herzegovina](/wiki/Bosnia_and_Herzegovina "Bosnia and Herzegovina"): [Alija Izetbegovic](/wiki/Alija_Izetbegovic "Alija Izetbegovic") espoused an Islamic inspired separatism)

## Governmental responses

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Separatism&action=edit&section=8 "Edit section: Governmental responses")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/9/94/The_pathway_of_regional_integration_or_separation.svg/500px-The_pathway_of_regional_integration_or_separation.svg.png)](/wiki/File:The_pathway_of_regional_integration_or_separation.svg)

Spectrum from separatism to [regional integration](/wiki/Regional_integration "Regional integration")

How far separatist demands will go toward full independence, and whether groups pursue constitutional and nonviolent action or armed violence, depend on a variety of economic, political, social and cultural factors, including movement leadership[[27]](#cite_note-27) and the government's response.[[4]](#cite_note-Horowitz-4) Governments may respond in a number of ways, some of which are mutually exclusive. Some include:[[28]](#cite_note-28)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/b/b5/The_coming_vote_-_Flickr_-_Al_Jazeera_English.jpg/250px-The_coming_vote_-_Flickr_-_Al_Jazeera_English.jpg)](/wiki/File:The_coming_vote_-_Flickr_-_Al_Jazeera_English.jpg)

[South Sudanese independence referendum](/wiki/2011_South_Sudanese_independence_referendum "2011 South Sudanese independence referendum") in 2011 marked the end of [South Sudan](/wiki/South_Sudan "South Sudan")'s long struggle for independence.

* accede to separatist demands, [independence](/wiki/Independence "Independence")
* improve the circumstances of disadvantaged minorities, be they religious, linguistic, territorial, economic or political
* adopt "asymmetric [federalism](/wiki/Federalism "Federalism")" where different states have different relations to the central government depending on separatist demands or considerations
* allow minorities to win in political disputes about which they feel strongly, through parliamentary voting, referendum, etc.
* settle for a [confederation](/wiki/Confederation "Confederation") or a [commonwealth](/wiki/Commonwealth "Commonwealth") relationship where there are only limited ties among states.
